perturbation.original_text: Each Party may disclose the other Party’s Confidential Information to its directors, officers, members, managers, employees, legal advisors, and financial advisors (each bound by obligations of confidentiality consistent with this Agreement) who have bona fide need to know, but only to the extent necessary to carry out the Business Purpose. Each Party agrees to instruct all such representatives not to disclose such Confidential Information to third parties, including consultants, without the prior written permission of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party may disclose the other Party’s Confidential Materials to its directors, officers, members, managers, employees, legal advisors, and financial advisors (each bound by obligations of confidentiality consistent with this Agreement) who have bona fide need to know, but only to the extent necessary to carry out the Business Purpose. Each Party agrees to instruct all such representatives not to disclose such Confidential Materials to third parties, including consultants, without the prior written permission of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Confidential Information' with 'Confidential Materials' in Section 4 introduces ambiguity, since 'Confidential Materials' is not a defined term, while 'Confidential Information' is. This creates an internal contradiction, as it's unclear whether these terms are the same and how to interpret the scope of permitted disclosure. Also Section 1 of the agreement defines Confidential Information and 'Confidential Materials' is not defined, creating confusion.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4. Disclosure to Representatives . Each Party may disclose the other Party’s Confidential Materials to its directors, officers, members, managers, employees, legal advisors, and financial advisors (each bound by obligations of confidentiality consistent with this Agreement) who have bona fide need to know, but only to the extent necessary to carry out the Business Purpose. Each Party agrees to instruct all such representatives not to disclose such Confidential Materials to third parties, including consultants, without the prior written permission of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The change replaces 'Confidential Information' (a defined term) with 'Confidential Materials' (an undefined term) in Section 4. Since the contradicted text is also Section 4, it is unclear whether 'Confidential Materials' has the same scope as 'Confidential Information,' which creates an internal contradiction, because it is unclear whether these terms are the same and how to interpret the scope of permitted disclosure. Also Section 1 of the agreement defines Confidential Information and 'Confidential Materials' is not defined, creating confusion. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall not include information which the Receiving Party is able to demonstrate by competent proof: (a) is now or hereafter becomes, through no act or omission on the part of the Receiving Party, generally known or available within the industry, or is now or later enters the public domain through no act or omission on the part of the Receiving Party; (b) was acquired or known by the Receiving Party before receiving such information from the Disclosing Party under this Agreement as shown by the Receiving Party’s written records; (c) is hereafter rightfully furnished to the Receiving Party by a third party having a right to disclose it, without restriction as to use or disclosure; (d) is information which the Receiving Party can document was independently developed by the Receiving Party without reference to Confidential Information furnished by the Disclosing Party; (e) is required by a court or other governmental authority of competent jurisdiction to be disclosed by the Receiving Party, provided the Receiving Party uses reasonable efforts to give the Disclosing Party reasonable notice of such required disclosure; or (f) is disclosed with the prior, written consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Proprietary Data shall not include information which the Receiving Party is able to demonstrate by competent proof: (a) is now or hereafter becomes, through no act or omission on the part of the Receiving Party, generally known or available within the industry, or is now or later enters the public domain through no act or omission on the part of the Receiving Party; (b) was acquired or known by the Receiving Party before receiving such information from the Disclosing Party under this Agreement as shown by the Receiving Party’s written records; (c) is hereafter rightfully furnished to the Receiving Party by a third party having a right to disclose it, without restriction as to use or disclosure; (d) is information which the Receiving Party can document was independently developed by the Receiving Party without reference to Confidential Information furnished by the Disclosing Party; (e) is required by a court or other governmental authority of competent jurisdiction to be disclosed by the Receiving Party, provided the Receiving Party uses reasonable efforts to give the Disclosing Party reasonable notice of such required disclosure; or (f) is disclosed with the prior, written consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'Confidential Information' with 'Proprietary Data' in Section 5, while not defining 'Proprietary Data,' it creates an ambiguity. This means that a previously defined term 'Confidential Information' is being used as the criteria on what a term that isn't defined is in the changed text, in section 5, leading to an in-text contradiction
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. Non-Confidential Information . Proprietary Data shall not include information which the Receiving Party is able to demonstrate by competent proof: (a) is now or hereafter becomes, through no act or omission on the part of the Receiving Party, generally known or available within the industry, or is now or later enters the public domain through no act or omission on the part of the Receiving Party; (b) was acquired or known by the Receiving Party before receiving such information from the Disclosing Party under this Agreement as shown by the Receiving Party’s written records; (c) is hereafter rightfully furnished to the Receiving Party by a third party having a right to disclose it, without restriction as to use or disclosure; (d) is information which the Receiving Party can document was independently developed by the Receiving Party without reference to Confidential Information furnished by the Disclosing Party; (e) is required by a court or other governmental authority of competent jurisdiction to be disclosed by the Receiving Party, provided the Receiving Party uses reasonable efforts to give the Disclosing Party reasonable notice of such required disclosure; or (f) is disclosed with the prior, written consent of the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Confidential Information.' The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Proprietary Data' in section 5, but does not define 'Proprietary Data.' This creates ambiguity because the conditions under which 'Proprietary Data' is considered non-confidential are based on the definition and characteristics of 'Confidential Information,' which is no longer directly linked to 'Proprietary Data' within the changed text. The 'Contradicted Text' section also uses this undefined 'Proprietary Data', further creating ambiguity because the document refers to it as if it has the same definition as 'Confidential Information', causing a contradiction as the document interchanges terms without defining them.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees not to remove any materials or tangible items containing any of the other party’s Confidential Information from the premises of the Disclosing Party without the Disclosing Party’s consent. Each Party agrees to comply with any and all terms and conditions that the Disclosing Party imposes upon approved removal of such materials or items, including, without limitation, that the removed materials or items must be returned by a certain date, and that no copies of the removed materials or items are to be made.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees not to remove any documents or tangible items containing any of the other party’s Confidential Information from the premises of the Disclosing Party without the Disclosing Party’s consent. Each Party agrees to comply with any and all terms and conditions that the Disclosing Party imposes upon approved removal of such materials or items, including, without limitation, that the removed materials or items must be returned by a certain date, and that no copies of the removed materials or items are to be made.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'materials' with 'documents' creates a contradiction because 'materials' is a broader term than 'documents.' This means that tangible items that aren't documents would not follow this rule, creating uncertainty. The contract refers to both 'materials' and 'items' implying different meanings, while 'documents' is too narrow and doesn't reflect the agreement intention. This means that in section 6, by only naming 'documents', the text now contradicts the original intent, since all other types of 'materials' and 'items' is not covered by this definition.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6. Removal of Confidential Materials . Each Party agrees not to remove any documents or tangible items containing any of the other party’s Confidential Information from the premises of the Disclosing Party without the Disclosing Party’s consent. Each Party agrees to comply with any and all terms and conditions that the Disclosing Party imposes upon approved removal of such materials or items, including, without limitation, that the removed materials or items must be returned by a certain date, and that no copies of the removed materials or items are to be made.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'materials' with 'documents'. The explanation states that 'materials' is broader. Section 6, the contradicted text, uses the new, narrower term 'documents'. This directly contradicts the original intent as 'materials' encompasses a wider range of items than 'documents', creating a contradiction. Since the contract uses both materials and items, replacing materials with documents leaves tangible items that are not documents uncovered by the rule, leading to in-text contradiction because of misaligned terminology.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s request, the Receiving Party will promptly return to the Disclosing Party all materials or tangible items containing the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information and all copies thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Disclosing Party’s request, the Receiving Party will promptly return to the Disclosing Party all information and copies thereof.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase "materials or tangible items containing the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information" and replacing it with just 'all information', creates a contradiction because it broadens the scope of what needs to be returned. While 'Confidential Information' has been defined, 'all information' hasn't. Leading to an in-text contradiction, since it's unclear what information is covered.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. Return of Confidential Information . Upon the Disclosing Party’s request, the Receiving Party will promptly return to the Disclosing Party all information and all copies thereof.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies the return of 'materials or tangible items containing the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information'. The changed text broadens this to 'all information'. The contradicted text also states 'all information'. However, 'all information' is undefined, whereas 'Confidential Information' is likely defined elsewhere in the contract. This creates an in-text contradiction because the scope of returnable items is now ambiguous, making it unclear what 'all information' encompasses, contradicting the implicit understanding that only defined 'Confidential Information' should be returned as per the original text. Therefore, it leads to uncertainty and potential disputes, which aligns with the Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party acknowledges that all of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential information is owned solely by the Disclosing Party (and/or its licensors) and that the unauthorized disclosure or use of such Confidential Information would cause irreparable harm and significant injury, the degree of which may be difficult to ascertain and for which monetary damages would provide an insufficient remedy. Accordingly, each Party agrees that the Disclosing Party shall have the right to obtain an immediate injunction from any court of competent jurisdiction enjoining actual or threatened breach of this Agreement and/or actual or threatened disclosure of the Confidential Information without the necessity of posting a bond or other security. Each Party shall also have the right to pursue any other rights or remedies available at law or equity for such a breach.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges that all of the Disclosing Party’s Secret information is owned solely by the Disclosing Party (and/or its licensors) and that the unauthorized disclosure or use of such Secret information would cause irreparable harm and significant injury, the degree of which may be difficult to ascertain and for which monetary damages would provide an insufficient remedy. Accordingly, each Party agrees that the Disclosing Party shall have the right to obtain an immediate injunction from any court of competent jurisdiction enjoining actual or threatened breach of this Agreement and/or actual or threatened disclosure of the Secret information without the necessity of posting a bond or other security. Each Party shall also have the right to pursue any other rights or remedies available at law or equity for such a breach.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the well-defined term "Confidential Information" with the undefined term "Secret information" creates a contradiction in Section 11, because Section 1 provides a full definition of "Confidential Information" but there is no definition of what "Secret Information" is. This makes it uncertain what information the parties intended to protect, as the remedies outlined are only for breaches related to 'Secret Information' now, the text now contradicts the original intent of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 11. Equitable and Legal Relief . Each Party acknowledges that all of the Disclosing Party’s Secret information is owned solely by the Disclosing Party (and/or its licensors) and that the unauthorized disclosure or use of such Secret information would cause irreparable harm and significant injury, the degree of which may be difficult to ascertain and for which monetary damages would provide an insufficient remedy. Accordingly, each Party agrees that the Disclosing Party shall have the right to obtain an immediate injunction from any court of competent jurisdiction enjoining actual or threatened breach of this Agreement and/or actual or threatened disclosure of the Secret information without the necessity of posting a bond or other security. Each Party shall also have the right to pursue any other rights or remedies available at law or equity for such a breach.
perturbation.justification: The original text refers to 'Confidential Information' which is a defined term within the contract. The changed text replaces 'Confidential Information' with 'Secret information,' which is not defined. This creates a direct contradiction because the contradicted section relies on the undefined term 'Secret information,' leading to ambiguity and unenforceability regarding what information is protected. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction,' as the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them, leading to conflicting obligations.
